exercises.translation: I must take medicine every day.
wrong_choice_explanations: なくてもいい means 'don't have to'; the opposite of obligation.
てはいけない means 'must not'; prohibition, not obligation.
たほうがいい means 'should'; a suggestion, not a strong obligation.

exercises.translation: I have to submit my homework.
wrong_choice_explanations: なくてもいい means 'don't need to submit'; opposite of must.
てもいい means 'it's okay to submit'; permission, not obligation.
ておく means 'do in advance'; preparation, not obligation.

exercises.translation: I must get up early tomorrow.
wrong_choice_explanations: たくない means 'don't want to'; expresses desire, not obligation.
てもいい means 'it's okay to get up early'; permission, not obligation.
たほうがいい means 'it would be better to'; advice, not necessity.

exercises.translation: You must participate in the meeting.
wrong_choice_explanations: なくてもいい means 'don't need to participate'; no obligation.
てはいけない means 'must not participate'; prohibition.
たがる means 'want to'; expresses desire, not necessity.

exercises.translation: You must be quiet in the library.
wrong_choice_explanations: なくてもいい means 'don't have to be quiet'; no obligation.
ばかりだ means 'just did'; indicates recent completion, not necessity.
つもりだ means 'intend to'; intention, not obligation.

exercises.example_sentence: このレポートは今日中に書か＿＿。
exercises.translation: This report must be written by the end of today.
wrong_choice_explanations: なくてもいい means 'doesn't have to be written'; no obligation.
てある means 'has been written'; indicates completed state, not obligation.
たほうがいい means 'it would be better to write'; suggestion, not must.

exercises.translation: I must get examined at the hospital.
wrong_choice_explanations: なくてもいい means 'don't have to get examined'; optional.
てしまう indicates completion or regret; not related to obligation.
ようになる means 'come to'; indicates gradual change, not necessity.
なくてもいい means 'don't have to follow'; no obligation.
てみる means 'try to follow'; indicates attempt, not must.
てはいけない means 'must not follow'; prohibition, which makes no sense.

exercises.example_sentence: 会社の面接の前に準備し＿＿。
exercises.translation: I must prepare before the company interview.
wrong_choice_explanations: なくてもいい means 'don't need to prepare'; optional.
てみる means 'try to prepare'; indicates attempt, not obligation.
かもしれない means 'might'; expresses possibility, not necessity.

exercises.example_sentence: 18歳になるまで、お酒を飲んではいけない。
exercises.translation: You must not drink alcohol until you turn 18.
wrong_choice_explanations: なければならない means 'must drink'; opposite of the intended prohibition.
てもいい means 'it's okay to drink'; permission, not prohibition.
たがる means 'want to'; expresses desire, inappropriate for prohibition.
